questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.subsection: 2.2.2 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by substitution
questions.question: When solving a system by substitution, the result \(y = y\) appears. Which statement is true about the system?

options.C: The system has infinitely many solutions

questions.hint: An identity like \(y=y\) after substitution means the equations are dependent.
questions.explanation: An identity \(y = y\) indicates the two equations are multiples of each other. Option C correctly identifies infinitely many solutions. Option A would give a false statement like \(0=5\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.2 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by substitution
source_trace.content_anchor: y=y. Hence, the solution is infinite.

explanations_tiered.definition: A dependent system has infinitely many solutions; substitution yields an identity like \(y = y\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When substitution eliminates all variables and leaves a true statement, the equations represent the same line.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you end up with something like \(y = y\), that's always true, so every point on the line works — infinite solutions.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The identity \(y = y\) means the two equations are equivalent, so the system has infinitely many solutions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Algebraically, \(y = y\) is a tautology, indicating the two equations are linearly dependent and describe coincident lines.
reasoning_steps: Solve one equation for a variable.
If all variables cancel leaving a true statement, the system has infinitely many solutions.
misconceptions: Confusing identity \(y=y\) with contradiction \(0=5\); identity means infinite solutions, contradiction means none.

glossary_entry.definition: An equation that is true for all values of the variable.

flashcard.front: What does \(y = y\) after substitution mean?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does \(y = y\) after substitution mean?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Identity means infinitely many solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.subsection: 2.2.3 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by Elimination method
questions.question: Solve using elimination: \(\begin{cases} 3x + y = 11 \\ x - 2y = 6 \end{cases}\). What is the value of \(x\)?
questions.hint: Multiply the second equation by \(-3\) to eliminate \(x\), or solve for one variable.
questions.explanation: Multiply second eq by \(-3\): \(-3x + 6y = -18\). Add to first: \((3x+y)+(-3x+6y)=11-18\) → \(7y = -7\) → \(y=-1\). Substitute back: \(3x + (-1) = 11\) → \(3x=12\) → \(x=4\). Option C correct.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.3 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by Elimination method
source_trace.content_anchor: Solve the following using substitution (elimination) method. Example 3 system

explanations_tiered.definition: Elimination method adds or subtracts equations after multiplying to cancel one variable.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Multiply the second equation by \(-3\) to make coefficients of \(x\) opposites, then add to eliminate \(x\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Make the \(x\)'s cancel by multiplying the second equation by \(-3\), then add the equations to find \(y\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Choosing to eliminate \(x\) first simplifies the system; back-substitution yields \(x=4, y=-1\), the unique intersection point.
reasoning_steps: Multiply equations to get opposite coefficients.
Solve for remaining variable, then back-substitute.
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply the entire equation, including the constant term.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Elimination: make coefficients opposite, then add.

glossary_entry.definition: Solving systems by adding/subtracting equations to remove a variable.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Method cancels a variable by adding equations.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show both elimination and substitution for same system to compare.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(2|2x-1| - 1 = 5\). What is the solution set?
questions.hint: Isolate the absolute value: \(|2x-1| = 3\), then split into two equations.
questions.explanation: Add 1: \(2|2x-1| = 6\) → \(|2x-1| = 3\). Then \(2x-1 = 3\) → \(x=2\), and \(2x-1 = -3\) → \(x=-1\). Option D lists both. Option C reverses the signs.

explanations_tiered.definition: First isolate the absolute value expression before splitting into two linear equations.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add 1, divide by 2 to get \(|2x-1| = 3\), then solve \(2x-1 = 3\) and \(2x-1 = -3\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Get the absolute value alone on one side first, then break into two regular equations.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The absolute value function is piecewise; isolating it first ensures correct case splitting without extraneous operations.
misconceptions: Distributing the 2 inside the absolute value before isolating, leading to \(|4x-2| = 6\) incorrectly.
Forgetting to divide the RHS by 2 before splitting.

glossary_entry.definition: Algebraic step to get |expression| alone before solving cases.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise order of operations: isolate absolute value before case splitting.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Isolating |2x-1|=3 gives x=2 and x=-1.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Factor and solve \(x^2 - 6x - 16 = 0\). Which pair of numbers gives the correct factors?
questions.hint: Find two numbers whose product is \(-16\) and sum is \(-6\): \(-8\) and \(+2\).
questions.explanation: Numbers \(-8\) and \(2\) multiply to \(-16\) and add to \(-6\), giving factors \((x-8)(x+2)=0\). Option A is correct. Option B gives sum 0, not -6.

explanations_tiered.definition: Factoring a quadratic finds two binomials whose product equals the original expression.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Need two numbers with product \(-16\) and sum \(-6\): \(-8\) and \(2\). Thus \((x-8)(x+2)=0\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find two numbers that multiply to \(-16\) and add to \(-6\); they are \(-8\) and \(+2\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: We need factors of \(-16\) that sum to \(-6\): \(-8\) and \(2\). So \((x-8)(x+2)=0\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The quadratic factors as \((x-8)(x+2)\); the zero-product property yields roots \(8\) and \(-2\).
reasoning_steps: Find two numbers with product \(c\) and sum \(b\).
Set each factor to zero and solve.
misconceptions: Choosing numbers that multiply to \(c\) but not checking sum.
Sign errors: \(-8\) and \(+2\) multiply to \(-16\) and sum to \(-6\) (correct).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Find factors of \(c\) that add to \(b\).

glossary_entry.definition: Writing quadratic as product of two binomials.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Systematically list factor pairs to avoid guessing.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Use the quadratic formula to solve \(x^2 - 6x + 3 = 0\). What are the roots?
questions.explanation: \(x = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36-12}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{24}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm 2\sqrt{6}}{2} = 3 \pm \sqrt{6}\). Option A simplifies correctly. Option B does not match the discriminant.

explanations_tiered.definition: The quadratic formula solves any quadratic equation \(ax^2+bx+c=0\), even when factoring is difficult.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Plug \(a=1, b=-6, c=3\) into \(x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}\) and simplify the radical.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Substitute the numbers into the formula, simplify what's under the square root, then reduce the fraction.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The discriminant \(24\) is not a perfect square, so the roots are irrational but real: \(3+\sqrt{6}\) and \(3-\sqrt{6}\).
misconceptions: Forgetting to simplify the radical \(\sqrt{24}\) to \(2\sqrt{6}\).

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply quadratic formula to solve equations

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For the quadratic \(ax^2 + 6x + c = 0\), the sum of roots is \(-\frac{6}{5}\) and product is \(\frac{1}{5}\). What is the value of \(a\)?
questions.hint: Sum of roots \(= -b/a\), product \(= c/a\). Here \(b=6\), so \(-6/a = -6/5\).
questions.explanation: Sum \(= -b/a = -6/a = -6/5\) → \(a = 5\). Option B correct. Product \(c/a = 1/5\) confirms \(c=1\).

explanations_tiered.definition: Vieta's formulas relate sum and product of roots to coefficients without solving the quadratic.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Sum \(= -b/a = -6/a\). Set equal to \(-6/5\) and solve for \(a\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Use the formula that says sum of roots equals \(-b/a\). Here \(b=6\), so \(-6/a = -6/5\), meaning \(a=5\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given \(-b/a = -6/5\) and \(b=6\), we have \(-6/a = -6/5\) → cross-multiplying gives \(-30 = -6a\) → \(a=5\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vieta's formulas provide direct coefficient relationships: \(-6/a = -6/5\) implies \(a=5\), independent of \(c\).

glossary_entry.definition: Relations between roots and coefficients of polynomial.

flashcard.front: If sum of roots = \(-6/5\) for \(ax^2+6x+c=0\), find \(a\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find \(a\) given sum of roots = \(-6/5\) in \(ax^2+6x+c=0\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that sum formula uses \(-b/a\), not \(b/a\).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use sum of roots to find coefficients

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(3^x = \frac{1}{27}\). What is the value of \(x\)?
questions.explanation: \(\frac{1}{27} = 3^{-3}\), so \(3^x = 3^{-3}\) → \(x = -3\). Option B correct. Option A gives positive 3, which is \(27\), not \(1/27\).

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rewrite RHS as power of 3: \(1/27 = 1/3^3 = 3^{-3}\). Then equate exponents.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(3^x = 3^{-3}\) → since bases are equal and positive, exponents must be equal: \(x = -3\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The exponential function \(f(x)=3^x\) is one-to-one, so equality of outputs implies equality of inputs.
worked_solution: Step 1: Express RHS as power of 3: \(1/27 = 1/3^3 = 3^{-3}\).
reasoning_steps: Write both sides with same base.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Connect negative exponents to fraction conversion.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve exponential equations with negative exponents

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Solve \(\sqrt{2x+3} + \sqrt{x-2} = 4\). After solving, which value is the only valid solution?
questions.hint: Isolate one radical, square, isolate again, square again, solve quadratic, then check domain \(x \geq 2\).
questions.explanation: After isolating and squaring twice, solutions are \(x=3\) and \(x=83\). Check domain: \(x \geq 2\) and also \(4 - \sqrt{x-2} \geq 0\). Only \(x=3\) satisfies all conditions. Option B correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: x=3 is the only solution, x=83 cannot be a solution

explanations_tiered.definition: Equations with two radicals require isolating one radical, squaring, then isolating the remaining radical and squaring again.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Isolate \(\sqrt{2x+3} = 4 - \sqrt{x-2}\), square, isolate radical again, square, solve quadratic \(x^2 - 86x + 249 = 0\), then check domain.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Move one square root to the other side, square, then repeat to get rid of all square roots. Finally, check which answers work in the original equation.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: After squaring twice: \(x^2 - 86x + 249 = 0\) → \((x-3)(x-83)=0\). Check \(x \geq 2\) and \(4 - \sqrt{x-2} \geq 0\); only \(x=3\) works.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Squaring introduces extraneous solutions; \(x=83\) fails the condition \(4 - \sqrt{x-2} \geq 0\) (RHS becomes negative), so it is extraneous.
reasoning_steps: Isolate one radical on one side.
Check all solutions in original equation and domain.
misconceptions: Forgetting to check domain constraints from each radical.
Not isolating radical before second squaring.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Two radicals → isolate, square, isolate again, square, check.

glossary_entry.definition: Equation containing two radical expressions, requiring two squaring steps.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate step-by-step isolation and squaring process.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Only x=3 satisfies original equation and domain.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Twice a son's age added to the father's age gives 56. Twice the father's age added to the son's age gives 82. What is the father's age?
questions.hint: Let son = \(s\), father = \(f\). Equations: \(2s + f = 56\) and \(s + 2f = 82\). Solve system.
questions.explanation: Solve system: multiply first by 2: \(4s+2f=112\), subtract second: \((4s+2f)-(s+2f)=112-82\) → \(3s=30\) → \(s=10\). Then \(2(10)+f=56\) → \(f=36\). Option D correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: If twice the age of a son is added to age of a father... find the ages

explanations_tiered.definition: Age problems use linear equations where variables represent current ages, with relationships given in words.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Set up system: \(2s+f=56\) and \(s+2f=82\). Solve by elimination to find \(f=36\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Translate words into two equations: twice son + father = 56, son + twice father = 82. Solve to find father's age.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Eliminate \(s\): multiply first eq by 2: \(4s+2f=112\), subtract second \(s+2f=82\) to get \(3s=30\), \(s=10\). Then \(f=56-20=36\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Linear system solution yields unique ages; cross-check: \(2(10)+36=56\) and \(10+2(36)=82\).
reasoning_steps: Solve system using elimination or substitution.
misconceptions: Solving only one equation and assuming linear relationship.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Translate words to equations, solve system.

glossary_entry.definition: Word problem using linear equations to find current ages.

publishing_metadata.revision_note: Set up equations carefully, solve system.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Encourage checking solution in both original statements.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve age problems using linear systems

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.subsection: 2.2.2 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by substitution
questions.structural_anchor: Steps in solving systems of linear equations in two variables by substitution
questions.question: When solving a system by substitution, after substituting, you get \(0 = 0\). What is the correct interpretation?

options.B: The system has exactly one solution

questions.hint: An identity like \(0=0\) means the two equations represent the same line.
questions.explanation: An identity \(0=0\) after substitution indicates the equations are dependent (coincident lines). Option C is correct. Option A would give a false statement like \(0=5\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.2 Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.2 Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by substitution
source_trace.structural_element: Steps in solving systems of linear equations in two variables by substitution
source_trace.content_anchor: Example 2: y=y. Hence, the solution is infinite.

explanations_tiered.definition: A dependent system has infinitely many solutions; the equations are multiples of each other.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When substitution yields \(0=0\), the two equations are equivalent, representing the same line.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you get \(0=0\), that's always true, so every point on the line is a solution — infinite answers.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(0=0\) is an identity, indicating the original equations are dependent and have infinitely many solutions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Algebraically, \(0=0\) is a tautology; geometrically, the lines coincide, yielding infinite intersection points.
reasoning_steps: If all variables cancel leaving a true statement, the system has infinitely many solutions (dependent).
misconceptions: Confusing identity \(0=0\) (infinite solutions) with contradiction \(0=5\) (no solution).

glossary_entry.definition: System where equations represent the same line.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Identity means dependent system.
